name: Inti
description: Inti, in Incan mythology, is the revered sun god, considered one of the most significant deities in the Incan pantheon. He was the divine ancestor of the Incan royal family and a crucial figure in their cosmological beliefs. Inti was not merely a celestial body but a powerful, active force responsible for warmth, light, and agricultural prosperity. The Inca believed that Inti sustained life, ensuring the growth of crops and the well-being of the people. His worship was central to Incan state religion, with elaborate ceremonies and temples dedicated to honoring him. As a benevolent provider, Inti was often depicted with a human face surrounded by radiant golden rays, symbolizing his divine power and life-giving energy. The Sapa Inca, the Incan ruler, was considered Inti's direct descendant, solidifying the divine right to rule and connecting the earthly realm to the celestial.
subtitle: The Sun God

keyMyths.title: Inti's Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: According to myth, Inti, feeling pity for the state of the world, sent his son and daughter to Earth to civilize humanity. They emerged from Lake Titicaca and taught the people agriculture, weaving, and other essential skills, laying the foundation for Incan society. This narrative underscores Inti's role as a benefactor and originator of Incan culture, highlighting his active involvement in shaping the world.
keyMyths.source: Bauer, Brian S. *The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cusco Region.* University of Texas Press, 1998.

keyMyths.title: The Festival of Inti Raymi
keyMyths.description: Inti Raymi, the Festival of the Sun, was the most important religious celebration in the Incan Empire. Held annually during the winter solstice, it was a grand ceremony to honor Inti and ensure the sun's return. Sacrifices, dances, and prayers were offered to appease the sun god and request his continued blessings for a bountiful harvest and the well-being of the Incan people. This festival demonstrates the central role of Inti in the Incan religious and agricultural calendar.
keyMyths.source: D'Altroy, Terence N. *The Incas*. Blackwell Publishing, 2002.

keyMyths.title: Inti and the Eclipse
keyMyths.description: Solar eclipses were viewed with great fear and anxiety by the Inca, who believed they signaled Inti's displeasure or even his death. During an eclipse, people would make loud noises, beat drums, and offer sacrifices to encourage Inti to return to his full glory. This fear underscores the Inca's dependence on Inti's constant presence and the precariousness they felt when his light was temporarily diminished.
keyMyths.source: Silverblatt, Irene. *Moon, Sun, and Witches: Gender Ideologies and Class in Inca and Colonial Peru*. Princeton University Press, 1987.

sources.author: Bauer, Brian S.
sources.work: The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cusco Region
sources.description: Explores the religious significance of the Cusco region and the role of Inti in the Incan worldview.
sources.text: Explores the religious significance of the Cusco region and the role of Inti in the Incan worldview.

sources.author: D'Altroy, Terence N.
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Incan history, culture, and religion, including detailed information on Inti.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Incan history, culture, and religion, including detailed information on Inti.

sources.author: Silverblatt, Irene
sources.work: Moon, Sun, and Witches: Gender Ideologies and Class in Inca and Colonial Peru
sources.description: Examines the gendered symbolism of the sun and moon in Incan society and the impact of colonialism on these beliefs.
sources.text: Examines the gendered symbolism of the sun and moon in Incan society and the impact of colonialism on these beliefs.
